original_abstract: This paper examines the nuance of linguistic "spin" in the addresses delivered by Barack Obama and Hassan Rouhani at the United Nations in September 2013. Drawing upon Halliday’s Systemic Functional Linguistics, the study utilizes transitivity and modality analyses to illustrate how both leaders leveraged language to weave ideology and power into their rhetoric. By deconstructing these linguistic choices, the paper demonstrates how the presidents articulated their authority, national capabilities, and strategic policies. Ultimately, this research seeks to clarify the underlying political implications of these speeches, making the subtle maneuvers of diplomatic discourse more accessible to a general audience.
humanized_abstract: This paper aims to study the linguistic mechanism of spin in speeches given by two leading statesmen in the international arena, Barack Obama and Hassan Rouhani, at the UN in September 2013. Based on the theories of Systemic Functional Linguistics (SFL) proposed by Halliday, the study applies the approaches of transitivity and modality analyses to investigate the use of linguistic resources by the two presidents to construct their ideologies and exercise their power in their speeches and to express their state power, national capacity and policy strategy in order to clarify the political implications of speeches given by leaders at the UN General Debates and to show the audience the techniques and tactics used by them to realise their power and influence in the international community through linguistic resources.
